title: Topic 12: Why Pro Players Risk Everything to Cheat: High-Stakes Cheating in Esports
content:
# Topic 12: Why Pro Players Risk Everything to Cheat: High-Stakes Cheating in Esports

## Abstract

This paper delves into the seemingly paradoxical world of professional esports, where athletes who have dedicated countless hours to achieving digital supremacy decide to employ the digital equivalent of a corked bat. We explore the compelling question of why these high-performers, perched at the apex of their careers, would risk reputation, fortune, and glory for an illicit advantage. The analysis posits that this phenomenon is not merely a matter of moral failing, but a complex interplay of crushing psychological pressure, incentive structures that inadvertently reward a "win-at-all-costs" mentality, and fundamental human drivers that, upon closer inspection, bear an uncanny resemblance to behaviors observed in everyday consumerism. In essence, the journey from pro-player to cheater is a cautionary tale written in broken code, revealing as much about the human condition as it does about the games we play.

## Introduction

I still remember the palpable tension in the living room during a particularly heated game of Monopoly. My uncle, a man who prided himself on his business acumen, was on a losing streak. With a subtle flick of the wrist, a crisp $500 bill was liberated from the bank and absorbed into his personal coffers. The ensuing chaos, a flurry of accusations and denials, was, in its own way, a microcosm of high-stakes competition. Now, substitute the board for a multi-million dollar stadium, the plastic houses for lucrative sponsorships, and my uncle for a twenty-year-old with lightning-fast reflexes, and you have the world of professional esports. It is a world where the stakes are astronomically higher, and the temptation to cheat, to find that digital shortcut to victory, is an ever-present specter.

This paper addresses a fascinating and increasingly relevant issue: high-stakes cheating in the burgeoning world of esports. We will venture beyond the simple condemnation of these acts to ask a more nuanced question: *why*? What drives a professional player, who has invested a significant portion of their life to mastering a game, to risk it all for an unfair advantage? The answer, we will find, is a rich tapestry woven from threads of psychology, economics, and the very nature of competition itself. We will begin by reviewing the existing academic literature on the psychology of cheating, with a particular focus on Self-Determination Theory, to provide a theoretical framework for our investigation. We will then dissect the anatomy of a cheat, exploring the immense pressures and skewed incentives that define the professional gaming landscape. To ground our analysis in reality, we will examine a series of real-world case studies, from the brazenly obvious to the cunningly sophisticated. Finally, we will discuss the broader implications of our findings, connecting the dots between cheating in esports and the patterns of human consumer behavior. This exploration will not only shed light on a unique subculture but also hold up a mirror to our own competitive impulses and the lengths we will go to in the pursuit of victory.

## Literature Review: The Ghosts in the Machine

Before we dive headfirst into the murky depths of esports scandals, it is only proper that we first consult the academics, those intrepid explorers of the human psyche who have attempted to map the treacherous terrain of cheating. One of the most illuminating frameworks for understanding this behavior comes from the world of psychology, specifically Self-Determination Theory (SDT). In essence, SDT posits that human beings have three fundamental psychological needs, the satisfaction of which leads to optimal functioning and well-being: **autonomy**, **competence**, and **relatedness** [1]. Think of it as the holy trinity of not hating what you do for a living. Autonomy is the need to feel in control of one's own behaviors and goals. Competence is the need to feel effective and skilled in one's endeavors. And relatedness is the need to feel connected to others. When these needs are met, we are more likely to be driven by **intrinsic motivation**—the pure, unadulterated joy of the activity itself. When they are thwarted, we become more susceptible to the siren song of **extrinsic motivation**—the bling and the cha-ching of external rewards.

A recent study on game cheating provides a compelling application of this theory [1]. The researchers found that players with high intrinsic motivation, those who played for the love of the game, were significantly less likely to cheat. Conversely, those driven by extrinsic factors, such as winning at all costs to gain rewards and status, were far more inclined to wander down the dark path of digital deception. The study also revealed a fascinating wrinkle: while autonomy and relatedness were found to boost intrinsic motivation and thus reduce cheating, competence, surprisingly, had no significant effect. This suggests that simply being good at a game is not enough to inoculate a player against the temptation to cheat. It is the *why* behind their play, not just the *how*, that truly matters.

This brings us to the concept of the "magic circle," a term coined by historian Johan Huizinga to describe the sacred space in which a game is played [2]. Within this circle, the rules of the game are paramount, and the outside world fades away. Cheating, in this context, is more than just breaking a rule; it is a fundamental violation of the magic circle, a shattering of the collective illusion that makes the game meaningful. When a player activates an aimbot or uses a map hack, they are not just giving themselves an unfair advantage; they are ripping a hole in the very fabric of the game, exposing the arbitrary nature of its rules and, in doing so, ruining the experience for everyone else. The academic literature, in its own dry and measured way, is telling us what every gamer already knows in their heart: cheaters are the worst.

## Analysis: Anatomy of a Cheat

The decision to cheat in professional esports is rarely a simple one. It is a complex calculus of risk and reward, driven by a potent cocktail of psychological pressures and economic incentives. To truly understand why a pro player would jeopardize their career, we must dissect the anatomy of this decision, peering into the mind of the high-stakes rule-breaker. At the heart of the matter lies the immense pressure to perform. The life of a professional gamer is not all glamour and glory; it is a relentless grind, a constant struggle to maintain a competitive edge in a field where the talent pool is constantly expanding and the slightest dip in performance can lead to obsolescence. This "pressure cooker" environment, as we might call it, creates a fertile ground for the seeds of cheating to take root. The fear of failure, the weight of expectations from teammates, sponsors, and a legion of fans—all of these factors can combine to create a psychological burden that becomes too heavy to bear.

In this high-pressure environment, cheating can begin to look like a rational, if unethical, solution to an intractable problem. It is a shortcut, a way to level the playing field, or even tilt it in one's favor. The allure of the unfair advantage is a powerful one, and it speaks to a fundamental aspect of human nature: our desire to find the path of least resistance. In the mind of the cheater, the act of installing a cheat program or exploiting a bug is not necessarily a malicious act of sabotage, but rather a twisted form of problem-solving. It is a game within the game, a secret metagame where the objective is not just to win, but to do so with an ace up one's sleeve. This mindset is further reinforced by the digital nature of the competition. The act of cheating in a video game can feel strangely disconnected from reality, a victimless crime committed in a world of pixels and code. This "digital disconnect" can lead to a form of moral myopia, where the ethical implications of one's actions are obscured by the virtual context in which they occur.

Of course, we cannot ignore the powerful influence of money. The economics of deceit are a crucial piece of the puzzle. With prize pools for major tournaments running into the millions of dollars, and with lucrative salaries and sponsorship deals on the line, the financial incentives to win are enormous. For a player who is struggling to make ends meet, or for one who is simply greedy for a larger share of the pie, the temptation to cheat can be overwhelming. A single victory, achieved through illicit means, could be the difference between a life of financial security and a return to obscurity. This is the dark side of the professionalization of gaming, a world where the passion for play can be corrupted by the pursuit of profit. When the extrinsic rewards of victory far outweigh the intrinsic satisfaction of fair competition, the stage is set for a tragedy of epic proportions, a digital drama where the hero, in their quest for glory, becomes the villain.

## Real-World Examples: A Hall of Shame

To move our discussion from the theoretical to the tangible, let us now venture into the digital trenches and examine a few cautionary tales from the annals of esports history. These stories, each a unique blend of hubris, ingenuity, and desperation, serve as vivid illustrations of the concepts we have explored thus far. Our first stop is the case of Nikhil "Forsaken" Kumawat, a Counter-Strike: Global Offensive player whose cheating was as blatant as it was brazen [3]. During a major tournament, Forsaken was caught red-handed with a cheat program on his computer, a file ironically named "word.exe." The subsequent investigation revealed a history of cheating, leading to a five-year ban and the dissolution of his team. The Forsaken scandal is a classic example of the "win-at-all-costs" mentality, a case where the desire for victory was so all-consuming that it led to a complete disregard for the rules of the game.

If the Forsaken case represents the brute-force approach to cheating, our next example, the PUBG "radar hack" scandal, showcases a more sophisticated and insidious form of deception. In this instance, multiple professional players were discovered to be using a hack that displayed the locations of their opponents on a second monitor [4]. This was not a simple aimbot, but a complex system that required a significant degree of technical savvy to implement. The radar hack scandal highlights the technological arms race that exists between cheaters and game developers, a constant cat-and-mouse game where each side is perpetually trying to outsmart the other. It also underscores the lengths to which players will go to gain an edge, even if it means transforming the game into a sterile exercise in digital surveillance.

Not all cheating is so cut and dry, however. The case of the League of Legends team Azubu Frost at the 2012 World Championship introduces a fascinating ethical gray area [5]. The team was fined for screen-peeking, looking at the large screens in the arena that displayed the game to the audience. While not a hack in the traditional sense, this act still provided them with an unfair advantage, allowing them to see their opponents' positions. The Azubu Frost incident raises important questions about the definition of cheating and the role of sportsmanship in a digital age. Finally, for a touch of the absurd, we turn to the world of virtual cycling and the case of a Zwift champion who was stripped of his title for "robo-doping" [6]. The player had used a bot to unlock a more powerful in-game bicycle, a digital form of performance enhancement that is as comical as it is contemptible. This bizarre tale serves as a potent reminder that the impulse to cheat knows no bounds, seeping into even the most unexpected corners of the competitive landscape.

## Discussion: So, What's the Point?

The phenomenon of high-stakes cheating in esports, while seemingly a niche issue, offers a surprisingly clear window into the broader patterns of human behavior, particularly in the realm of consumerism. The same psychological drivers that compel a professional gamer to install an aimbot are, in many ways, the same drivers that influence our purchasing decisions and our pursuit of status in the material world. The desire for a shortcut, for a "life hack" that promises maximum results with minimum effort, is a powerful force in our consumer culture. We are constantly bombarded with advertisements for products that promise to make us thinner, richer, and more popular, all with the simple swipe of a credit card. This is the consumerist equivalent of a cheat code, a way to bypass the hard work and dedication that are traditionally required to achieve success.

The gamification of life, the increasing tendency to apply game-like mechanics such as points, rewards, and leaderboards to real-world activities, further blurs the lines between play and reality. From fitness apps that award badges for completing a workout to loyalty programs that offer exclusive perks to top-spending customers, we are all, in a sense, players in a grand, consumerist game. And just as in the world of esports, this gamified environment can foster a "win-at-all-costs" mentality, where the pursuit of points and rewards becomes more important than the intrinsic value of the activity itself. When our self-worth becomes tied to our position on a leaderboard, the temptation to cheat, to find a way to game the system, can be difficult to resist.

For the esports industry, the implications of our analysis are clear. To combat the scourge of cheating, it is not enough to simply develop more sophisticated anti-cheat software. The industry must also address the underlying psychological and economic factors that create the incentive to cheat in the first place. This means fostering a culture that values intrinsic motivation, that celebrates the love of the game as much as the thrill of victory. It means creating a more supportive and less cutthroat environment for professional players, one where the fear of failure is not so all-consuming. And it means re-examining the incentive structures that, in their current form, may be inadvertently rewarding the very behavior they are trying to eliminate. The fight against cheating is not just a technological battle; it is a battle for the soul of esports.

## Conclusion

In the final analysis, the story of the high-stakes cheater in esports is a tale as old as time, a digital retelling of the age-old struggle between ambition and integrity. We have seen how the immense pressure to perform, the seductive allure of extrinsic rewards, and the very nature of the digital medium can conspire to turn a dedicated professional into a rule-breaker. The journey from the practice room to the hall of shame is a short and perilous one, paved with good intentions and compromised principles. But perhaps the most fascinating aspect of this phenomenon is not the act of cheating itself, but our enduring fascination with it. We are drawn to the stories of cheaters, both in games and in life, because they speak to a fundamental tension within the human spirit, a tension between our desire to win and our need to believe in the fairness of the game. The cheater, in their own twisted way, holds up a mirror to our own competitive impulses, forcing us to confront the uncomfortable truth that, given the right circumstances, we might all be tempted to take a shortcut. And so, the next time you find yourself on the verge of bankruptcy in a game of Monopoly, with the banker's back turned, ask yourself: what would a pro gamer do?
references:
[1] Lee, S. J., Jeong, E. J., Kim, D. J., & Kong, J. (2023). The influence of psychological needs and motivation on game cheating: insights from self-determination theory. *Frontiers in Psychology*, *14*, 1278738. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2023.1278738

[2] Huizinga, J. (2014). *Homo ludens: A study of the play-element in culture*. Beacon Press.

[3] Euronews. (2024, January 12). *Forsaken, how the 'hidden gem' of Indian esports was caught cheating*. https://www.euronews.com/next/2024/01/12/forsaken-how-the-hidden-gem-of-indian-esports-was-caught-cheating-euronews-tech-talks
